---
title: "The trace of a matrix"
date: "2026-06-09"
description: "Trace as a Bridge Between Item Space and Factor Space"
categories: [philosophie]
---

## Trace as a Bridge Between Item Space and Factor Space

One of the most important properties of the trace of a matrix is its **cyclic invariance**:

$$
\operatorname{tr}(ABC)
=
\operatorname{tr}(BCA)
=
\operatorname{tr}(CAB).
$$

In the context of factor analysis, this immediately implies

$$
\operatorname{tr}(\Lambda \Phi \Lambda^T)
=
\operatorname{tr}(\Lambda^T \Lambda \Phi).
$$

This identity has a deep geometric interpretation. In the common factor model

$$
\Sigma = \Lambda \Phi \Lambda^T + \Psi,
$$

the matrix

$$
\Sigma_c = \Lambda \Phi \Lambda^T
$$

represents the covariance structure explained by the common factors. It resides in the **item space** and has dimension $p \times p$, where $p$ denotes the number of observed variables.

Using the cyclic property of the trace, the same common variance can be written as

$$
\operatorname{tr}(\Lambda^T \Lambda \Phi).
$$

The matrix

$$
\Lambda^T \Lambda
$$

resides in the **factor space** and has dimension $m \times m$, where $m$ denotes the number of factors.

The equality of these two trace values shows that the total variance explained by the common factors remains the same regardless of whether it is viewed in the high-dimensional space of observed variables or in the lower-dimensional latent factor space. The trace therefore acts as a kind of *conservation law* for common variance.

Geometrically,

$$
\Lambda \Phi \Lambda^T
$$

describes the structure of the data in item space, whereas

$$
\Lambda^T \Lambda \Phi
$$

describes the same structure from the perspective of factor space. Although these matrices have different dimensions and represent different objects, they yield the same total amount of common variance.

### Connection to PCA and SVD

The same idea appears in principal component analysis (PCA) and singular value decomposition (SVD). For any matrix $A$,

$$
\operatorname{tr}(AA^T)
=
\operatorname{tr}(A^TA).
$$

The matrix $AA^T$ describes the geometry in one space, whereas $A^TA$ describes the same structure in a different coordinate system. Both matrices contain the same total variance, even though they may have different dimensions.

In PCA, the trace of the covariance matrix equals the sum of its eigenvalues and therefore the total variance of the data:

$$
\operatorname{tr}(\Sigma)
=
\sum_{i=1}^{p} \lambda_i.
$$

Factor analysis extends this idea to the common variance component. The identity

$$
\operatorname{tr}(\Lambda \Phi \Lambda^T)
=
\operatorname{tr}(\Lambda^T \Lambda \Phi)
$$

shows that the explained common variance is invariant under whether it is viewed in item space or factor space.

### Intuition

The factor loading matrix $\Lambda$ can be viewed as a linear mapping from factor space into item space. The matrix

$$
\Lambda \Phi \Lambda^T
$$

describes how factor variance is distributed across the observed variables. In contrast,

$$
\Lambda^T \Lambda \Phi
$$

describes how the same variance is organized within the latent factor space.

The trace reveals that no common variance is lost when moving between these two perspectives—it is merely represented in a different coordinate system. The cyclic invariance of the trace therefore provides the mathematical foundation for the duality between item space and factor space in factor analysis.
